lending.collateral.card1.text: Approval depends only on the value of the collateral you lock, not your history.
lending.collateral.card1.title: No credit check
lending.collateral.card2.text: You typically lock more value than you borrow, to absorb price swings.
lending.collateral.card2.title: Over collateralized
lending.collateral.card3.text: Unpaid collateral can be automatically sold by the protocol, without a court process.
lending.collateral.card3.title: Seizable
lending.collateral.p1: Collateral is an asset you lock in a lending protocol's smart contract to secure a loan. Instead of a credit check, the protocol relies entirely on the value of what you've locked up: if you stop repaying, the protocol can seize and sell your collateral to cover the debt.
lending.collateral.p2: Because crypto asset prices can move quickly, most lending protocols require over collateralization: the collateral you post is worth more than the amount you borrow, giving the protocol a buffer before a price drop puts the loan at risk.
lending.collateral.title: Collateral
lending.flashLoans.card1.text: The loan only exists within one transaction, so there's no time for the price to move against it.
lending.flashLoans.card1.title: No collateral required
lending.flashLoans.card2.text: If repayment fails at any point, the whole transaction reverts, and the loan never happened.
lending.flashLoans.card2.title: All or nothing
lending.flashLoans.closing: Flash loans have also been used to manipulate prices within a single transaction, which is one reason many protocols design carefully around them.
lending.flashLoans.p1: A flash loan lets you borrow any amount from a lending pool with no collateral at all, as long as you repay it, plus a fee, within the same transaction. If the loan isn't repaid before the transaction ends, the entire transaction is reversed as if it never happened.
lending.flashLoans.p2: This makes flash loans useful for tasks that need a large, temporary amount of capital: refinancing a position across protocols, or correcting a price difference between two markets, all inside a single atomic transaction.
lending.flashLoans.title: Flash Loans
lending.glossary.def1: A smart contract system that lets users supply assets to earn interest or borrow assets against collateral.
lending.glossary.def10: Debt that remains after a liquidation fails to fully cover it with the collateral seized.
lending.glossary.def2: An asset locked up to secure a loan, which can be seized if the loan isn't repaid.
lending.glossary.def3: Loan to Value: the ratio between what you've borrowed and the value of your collateral.
lending.glossary.def4: The formula a lending protocol uses to set borrowing and supply rates based on utilization.
lending.glossary.def5: The share of a lending pool's assets that are currently borrowed out.
lending.glossary.def6: A live score representing how close a borrowing position is to liquidation.
lending.glossary.def7: The forced sale of collateral when a loan's safety margin falls too low.
lending.glossary.def8: A participant, often an automated bot, who repays part of an unsafe loan in exchange for discounted collateral.
lending.glossary.def9: An uncollateralized loan that must be borrowed and repaid within a single transaction.
lending.glossary.title: Glossary
lending.healthFactor.above.text: Position is safe. The higher the number, the larger the price drop needed before liquidation.
lending.healthFactor.above.title: Above 1
lending.healthFactor.below.text: Position is eligible for liquidation. Anyone can trigger it and claim part of the collateral.
lending.healthFactor.below.title: At or below 1
lending.healthFactor.closing: A falling health factor is the clearest warning sign a borrower has that a position needs attention, before a liquidation happens automatically.
lending.healthFactor.p1: The health factor is a single live number summarizing how close a borrowing position is to liquidation. It's calculated from your collateral value, your borrowed amount, and the maximum LTV your collateral allows.
lending.healthFactor.p2: A health factor above 1 means the position is safe; a health factor at or below 1 means the collateral is no longer sufficient, and the position becomes eligible for liquidation.
lending.healthFactor.title: Health Factor
lending.hero.description: On chain lending markets that replace a bank's credit check with collateral, and pay interest that adjusts automatically as supply and demand shift.
lending.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
lending.hero.stat2.label: Duration
lending.hero.stat3.label: Format
lending.hero.stat4.label: Sections
lending.hero.title: Lending
lending.interestRateModels.borrow.text: What borrowers pay, rising as utilization increases toward full utilization.
lending.interestRateModels.borrow.title: Borrow Rate
lending.interestRateModels.closing: Because rates update in real time, the same lending market can offer very different terms from one week to the next.
lending.interestRateModels.p1: Interest rate models are the formulas a lending protocol uses to set borrowing and supplying rates automatically, based on how much of the pool's assets are currently borrowed.
lending.interestRateModels.p2: As utilization rises (more of the pool is borrowed out), rates increase for both borrowers and suppliers, encouraging new supply and discouraging further borrowing. As utilization falls, rates drop again.
lending.interestRateModels.supply.text: What suppliers earn, funded directly by the interest borrowers pay.
lending.interestRateModels.supply.title: Supply Rate
lending.interestRateModels.title: Interest Rate Models
lending.introduction.p1: Every DeFi activity covered in the last lesson, trading and providing liquidity, assumed you already owned the asset you were working with. Lending markets change that: they let you borrow assets you don't own, or earn interest by supplying assets you do own, all without a bank running a credit check.
lending.introduction.p2: This lesson explains how collateral replaces that credit check, how loan to value (LTV) sets your safety margin, how interest rate models adjust automatically with supply and demand, and what actually happens, step by step, when a position gets liquidated.
lending.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you will understand why lending protocols require over collateralization, how to read a health factor, what a flash loan actually is, and the specific risks that come with borrowing or supplying on chain.
lending.introduction.title: Introduction
lending.liquidations.closing: Liquidation discounts exist so liquidators are incentivized to act the moment a position becomes unsafe, protecting the protocol's solvency.
lending.liquidations.field1.label: Trigger
lending.liquidations.field2.label: Liquidator Repays
lending.liquidations.field3.label: Liquidator Receives
lending.liquidations.field4.label: Borrower Keeps
lending.liquidations.field4.value: Remaining collateral, minus the amount liquidated
lending.liquidations.p1: A liquidation is the forced sale of a borrower's collateral, triggered automatically once their health factor drops to or below 1. Liquidations happen through smart contracts, without a court process or a phone call.
lending.liquidations.p2: Liquidators, often automated bots, repay part of a borrower's debt and receive a portion of their collateral in return, usually at a discount as an incentive to act quickly.
lending.liquidations.title: Liquidations
lending.ltv.closing: The gap between your current LTV and the maximum allowed is exactly what the health factor measures, covered later in this lesson.
lending.ltv.field1.label: Collateral
lending.ltv.field2.label: Borrowed
lending.ltv.field3.label: LTV
lending.ltv.field4.label: Max LTV
lending.ltv.p1: Loan to value, or LTV, is the ratio between how much you've borrowed and the value of the collateral backing it. A lower LTV means a bigger safety buffer; a higher LTV means less room before the position becomes unsafe.
lending.ltv.p2: Every asset a protocol accepts as collateral has a maximum LTV it allows, set according to how volatile and liquid that asset is. More volatile assets get a lower maximum LTV, since their price can move against a position faster.
lending.ltv.title: Loan to Value (LTV)
lending.meta.title: Lending Fundamentals - Nodra Academy
lending.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
lending.quiz.q1: 1. Why don't lending protocols run a credit check?
lending.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Collateral and try again.
lending.quiz.q1o1: A. They rely entirely on the value of the collateral a borrower locks up
lending.quiz.q1o2: B. They verify identity through a government database instead
lending.quiz.q1o3: C. All loans are automatically insured against default
lending.quiz.q1o4: D. Borrowers must be pre-approved by the protocol's team
lending.quiz.q2: 2. What does a lower LTV give a borrower?
lending.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Loan to Value and try again.
lending.quiz.q2o1: A. A higher interest rate on their loan
lending.quiz.q2o2: B. A larger safety buffer before the position becomes unsafe
lending.quiz.q2o3: C. The ability to borrow without any collateral
lending.quiz.q2o4: D. Priority during a liquidation event
lending.quiz.q3: 3. What happens to interest rates as a lending pool's utilization rises?
lending.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Interest Rate Models and try again.
lending.quiz.q3o1: A. They stay fixed regardless of utilization
lending.quiz.q3o2: B. They rise for both borrowers and suppliers, encouraging new supply
lending.quiz.q3o3: C. They fall for borrowers and rise for suppliers
lending.quiz.q3o4: D. The protocol pauses new borrowing entirely
lending.quiz.q4: 4. What does supplying an asset to a lending pool let you do?
lending.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Supplying and Borrowing and try again.
lending.quiz.q4o1: A. Automatically borrow the maximum LTV available
lending.quiz.q4o2: B. Earn interest, funded by what borrowers pay
lending.quiz.q4o3: C. Avoid ever needing to post collateral
lending.quiz.q4o4: D. Bypass a protocol's smart contract entirely
lending.quiz.q5: 5. What does a health factor at or below 1 mean?
lending.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Health Factor and try again.
lending.quiz.q5o1: A. The position is completely safe from liquidation
lending.quiz.q5o2: B. The position is eligible for liquidation
lending.quiz.q5o3: C. The borrower has fully repaid their loan
lending.quiz.q5o4: D. The protocol has paused all withdrawals
lending.quiz.q6: 6. Who can trigger a liquidation, and what do they receive?
lending.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Liquidations and try again.
lending.quiz.q6o1: A. Only the protocol's own team, receiving a fee
lending.quiz.q6o2: B. Anyone, typically a bot, receiving discounted collateral
lending.quiz.q6o3: C. Only the original borrower, receiving a refund
lending.quiz.q6o4: D. A government regulator, receiving a penalty payment
lending.quiz.q7: 7. What makes a flash loan different from a normal loan?
lending.quiz.q7.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Flash Loans and try again.
lending.quiz.q7o1: A. It requires more collateral than a normal loan
lending.quiz.q7o2: B. It requires no collateral, but must be repaid within the same transaction
lending.quiz.q7o3: C. It can only be used to buy governance tokens
lending.quiz.q7o4: D. It has a fixed repayment period of 30 days
lending.quiz.q8: 8. What is "bad debt" in a lending protocol?
lending.quiz.q8.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Lending Risks and try again.
lending.quiz.q8o1: A. Interest that a borrower refuses to pay
lending.quiz.q8o2: B. Debt left uncovered after a liquidation fails to fully offset it with seized collateral
lending.quiz.q8o3: C. A loan that has been repaid ahead of schedule
lending.quiz.q8o4: D. A loan approved without any collateral requirement
lending.quiz.q9: 9. Why does oracle risk matter to a lending protocol?
lending.quiz.q9.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Lending Risks and try again.
lending.quiz.q9o1: A. Oracles set the protocol's governance rules
lending.quiz.q9o2: B. Manipulated price feeds can trigger unfair or premature liquidations
lending.quiz.q9o3: C. Oracles determine which assets can be supplied
lending.quiz.q9o4: D. Oracles have no effect on collateral valuation
lending.quiz.title: Quiz
lending.risks.intro: Lending on chain removes a bank from the process, but it introduces its own specific set of risks.
lending.risks.li1: Liquidation risk. A price move against your collateral can trigger a liquidation faster than you can react.
lending.risks.li2: Smart contract risk. A bug in the protocol's code can put deposited or borrowed funds at risk.
lending.risks.li3: Oracle risk. Lending protocols rely on price feeds to value collateral; a manipulated feed can trigger unfair liquidations.
lending.risks.li4: Interest rate risk. Rates can rise sharply during high utilization, increasing your borrowing cost with little warning.
lending.risks.li5: Bad debt risk. If collateral value falls faster than liquidators can act, a protocol can end up with debt that isn't fully covered by collateral.
lending.risks.title: Lending Risks
lending.summary.p1: Lending protocols replace a bank's credit check with collateral, letting anyone borrow against assets they've locked up or earn interest by supplying assets to a shared pool. Interest rate models adjust automatically as utilization rises and falls.
lending.summary.p2: A position's health factor tracks how close it is to liquidation, which happens automatically once collateral no longer sufficiently covers a loan. Flash loans push this model to its logical extreme: borrowing with no collateral at all, as long as repayment happens in the same transaction.
lending.summary.title: Summary
lending.supplyingBorrowing.closing: Nothing stops you from supplying and borrowing the same asset in different markets, though most people borrow something other than what they've posted as collateral.
lending.supplyingBorrowing.intro: Supplying means depositing an asset into a lending pool to earn interest; borrowing means taking assets out of that same pool against collateral you've posted. Most participants do both at once: supplying one asset as collateral while borrowing another against it.
lending.supplyingBorrowing.li1: Supplying. Deposit an asset, receive a token representing your deposit plus accrued interest.
lending.supplyingBorrowing.li2: Borrowing. Post collateral, then withdraw a different asset up to your allowed LTV.
lending.supplyingBorrowing.li3: Repaying. Return the borrowed asset plus interest to reclaim your collateral in full.
lending.supplyingBorrowing.title: Supplying and Borrowing
lending.toc.title: Contents
